1432423366.body:
From the AI vendors you use to the AI features you build, your risk surface is expanding faster than most programs were built to handle.

Today at Vanta Delivers, we introduced new capabilities that provide you a unified view across your risk program, so you can lean into AI with confidence. 

Here's what we announced ⬇️

The Agent for TPRM handles the heavy lifting on vendor assessments, gathering evidence, pre-filling questionnaires, and flagging responses that need a human to weigh in. Multiple Assessments per Vendor lets security, legal, privacy, and AI reviews run concurrently for the same vendor, each with their own owner, all rolling up into one decision. And Continuous Vendor Monitoring surfaces incidents the moment they're detected with enough context to act on them fast.

The Agent for Risk connects each risk to the assets, controls, and vendors associated with it, so risk owners can move from surfacing a problem to acting on it without starting from zero. That includes mapping risk to assets, vendors, and controls.

On the internal risk side, our AI risk library will give teams a structured starting point for governing AI tools already running inside their org. Factor-Based Inherent Scoring layers on, so you can assess risk across financial, operational, reputational, and other dimensions. And Automated Residual Risk Scoring updates your scores automatically as controls change.

Check out the highlight reel, then see more on our latest updates: https://lnkd.in/e_Jvmpfr

1431601756.body:
The numbers on shadow AI are worse than most people think. 

According to our own data, 70% of Vanta's 16K+ customers have AI tools running inside their organizations that haven't gone through formal security review. 

I met with leaders at a multinational GSI last week and they laughed at this and said "Understatement! Every company is suffering from this risk!"

Meanwhile, companies are only reviewing 7% of their AI vendors, even though 30% of them are critical or high risk. And 88% of risks go unremediated. Yikes. 😬 

We built the Vanta Agent for Risk because the way organizations work has fundamentally changed, and the tools security teams use to manage risk haven't kept up. 

The agent maps vendors, tools, data, controls, and compliance responsibilities into one continuously updated picture, so the risks that were previously invisible become visible, and actionable.

Harry McCracken at Fast Company spent time with our team digging into all of it: 

https://lnkd.in/ge-jetAW
1431601756.headline: 'Shadow AI' is real. Vanta wants to help manage it

1424684176.body:
Enterprise risk is never static. So why are we using static GRC tools?

In the traditional GRC setup, you have all your risks listed with ratings inside the platform. Your risk analyst logs in to go through them and figure out what needs a deeper dig.

The analyst needs situational awareness to tie all the pieces together and give you the right insight.

In the legacy platforms, your team only gets a static snapshot of your environment, which means you only see what was caught at that moment.

Here's what's different when you have an AI agent at your fingertips:

✨ Continuous monitoring means you know what's happening in engineering right now and where controls are falling short
💡  Deep data processing means you can compare policies against what is actually being followed
🌎 Context across data sources means you can surface risks you haven’t even thought to document in your registry yet

AI is not smarter than your human analyst. It just doesn't have to choose what to pay attention to, the processing power is the real unlock, the agent is watching everything, all the time.

That’s the direction we're heading at Vanta.

The AI agent that looks at all the data, gives you visibility into a constantly changing environment and tells you where something's missing, helping you along the way to prioritize the right risks and make the right decisions. 

As we figure out how to extend trust wisely to AI and allow it to power the security team the same way it's powering the rest of the organization.....we enable a new approach to enterprise risk management. 

Check out more of what we are up to here: https://lnkd.in/e2b8SBuM

In the traditional GRC setup, you have all your risks listed with ratings inside the platform. Your risk analyst logs in to go through them and figure out what needs a deeper dig.

The analyst needs situational awareness to tie all the pieces together and give you the right insight.

In the legacy platforms, your team only gets a static snapshot of your environment, which means you only see what was caught at that moment.

Here's what's different when you have an AI agent at your fingertips:

✨ Continuous monitoring means you know what's happening in engineering right now and where controls are falling short
💡  Deep data processing means you can compare policies against what is actually being followed
🌎 Context across data sources means you can surface risks you haven’t even thought to document in your registry yet

AI is not smarter than your human analyst. It just doesn't have to choose what to pay attention to, the processing power is the real unlock, the agent is watching everything, all the time.

That’s the direction we're heading at Vanta.

The AI agent that looks at all the data, gives you visibility into a constantly changing environment and tells you where something's missing, helping you along the way to prioritize the right risks and make the right decisions. 

As we figure out how to extend trust wisely to AI and allow it to power the security team the same way it's powering the rest of the organization.....we enable a new approach to enterprise risk management. 

Check out more of what we are up to here: https://lnkd.in/e2b8SBuM

1194779943.body:
How would Thoropass have helped me earlier in my compliance and risk career? Simple: visibility. You can’t manage risk you cannot see.

More than a decade ago at Citi, I didn’t have real-time transparency into our control environment. I had stale data. Static reports. Lagging indicators.

If there was a vulnerability that could be exploited … or if a control wasn’t operating effectively … or if a process drifted from policy…

I wouldn’t know in the moment. I would know six weeks after month-end. Six weeks is an eternity in risk management. It’s incredibly difficult to govern effectively when visibility is delayed. You’re reacting instead of steering. 

Real-time transparency changes that. With continuous visibility and control monitoring, you can:
- Identify vulnerabilities earlier
- Understand whether controls are actually operating as designed
- Mitigate risk in real time — not retroactively

That’s the difference between reporting on risk and actually managing it. This is the compliance experience I wish I had earlier in my career.

1122187913.body:
How can AI support compliance professionals in a meaningful way?

This is something many of us at Thoropass have a front-row seat to every day.

AI has the potential to completely reshape how compliance teams operate. Not by replacing them, but by freeing them to focus on what actually moves the needle.

Certain parts of the job like audit prep, evidence collection, writing controls, mapping requirements, drafting remediation plans are time-consuming. They're  manual, and frankly, preventable. I like to call them BUSY WORK.

AI is the most effective weapon we have to stop busy work from draining your team’s time, energy, and morale.

When used intentionally, AI enables three structural shifts that define the future of compliance:

1. Eliminate the operational drag.
Automating evidence reviews, control mapping, and repeatable workflows gives teams back dozens (sometimes hundreds) of hours. That reclaimed time isn’t just efficiency — it’s the difference between “keeping up” and actually advancing the program.

2. Shift from firefighting to foresight.
With more time and cleaner data, compliance teams can finally operate proactively: spotting emerging risks sooner, strengthening controls before issues escalate, and partnering with the business instead of reacting to it.
 AI turns compliance from a checklist function into a strategic one.

3. Build resilience against an AI-powered threat landscape.
It’s not just defenders using AI — attackers are too. The speed, volume, and sophistication of threats are accelerating. Teams that adopt AI don’t just work faster; they see patterns earlier, respond quicker, and can handle risks that would overwhelm traditional processes.

The takeaway:
AI isn’t just a tool to simplify work or speed up existing processes.
It’s what finally breaks the cycle of busy work that has held compliance teams back for decades — unlocking the time, insight, and resilience needed to operate at a truly strategic level in an increasingly complex risk environment.

1115925313.body:
When compliance becomes theater, everyone loses.

Lately I’ve heard a story circulating in our industry:
Identical SOC 2 reports generated for completely different companies — rubber-stamped by audit firms that hadn’t even passed peer review.

Whether every detail is true almost doesn’t matter.

What does matter is that no one seems too shocked.

And that’s the real problem.

Somewhere along the way, parts of compliance turned audits into theater - optimized ONLY for speed and cost, not trust and assurance. When that happens, security becomes performative. Reports look official. Confidence feels real. But the substance isn’t there.

Automation is a friend.

Cutting corners is the enemy.

AI should make audits better, not emptier.

Compliance and assurance should be about trust, validation, and accountability.

If you take things seriously, a SOC 2 report becomes a painless byproduct of doing security well.

The future of compliance won’t belong to whoever just moves the fastest.

It will belong to those who restore trust by raising the bar.

At Thoropass, that’s the hill we’re willing to stand on.

969420993.body:
In 2013, I saw a seismic shift coming in how enterprises would evaluate third parties. Long before anyone else was talking about it.

I was leading IT Risk & Control for Citi’s Institutional business, managing over 10,000 third parties. New OCC and Fed guidance made one thing clear: enterprises could no longer rely on outdated oversight. Every third party needed to demonstrate strong security and compliance posture—and prove it through high-quality audits and certifications.

The challenge was massive: fragmented tools, no unified view, and no real-time visibility into how third parties were performing against regulatory and internal requirements. 

Ongoing monitoring was nearly impossible, and enterprises didn’t have endless budgets to rise to the compliance bar—especially as the threat landscape kept evolving.

That experience shaped everything that became Thoropass. The solution had to start with third parties: giving them tools, automation, and embedded audits to continuously meet enterprise diligence standards, freeing internal teams to focus on higher-value work.

Today, Thoropass delivers:

-Ongoing monitoring of third-party compliance
-High-quality audits integrated into the workflow
-Automation that reduces manual work while keeping up with regulatory and security demands

The 2013 wake-up call wasn’t just about regulation. It was about building a system that ensures third parties can keep pace with the speed, scale, and risk of modern business.

969340983.body:
Most audit firms sound the same from the outside.

Same claims. Same promises. Same “ex-Big 4 talent.”

But after leading IT Risk at Citi and later scaling Thoropass, one thing has become clear:

The real difference in audit quality comes from judgment, consistency, and a delivery model built to scale knowledge, not lose it year after year.

Here’s what actually separates audit partners that deliver from those that don’t:

1️⃣ Talent + a scalable solution

Every firm sells “great auditors.” That should be table stakes (more on that topic in another post).

The real differentiator? Institutional knowledge that compounds and a solution that captures it.

With Thoropass, you get:
-One audit team that stays with you
-Context that carries forward year after year
-A system that documents and transfers knowledge, so it’s never trapped in one person’s inbox

Year two is smoother than year one. And year three? It’s even better.

People + technology = faster, smarter outcomes.

2️⃣ Approach

Traditional audits still run like it’s 2010:
-Manual evidence gathering
-Endless emails
-Multi-month timelines

We do it differently:
-200+ integrations pull evidence automatically
-Multi-framework alignment instead of pursuing frameworks individually
-AI handles the tedious work like initial review, matching, verification

Your auditor focuses on what matters: judgment, context, and decision-making.

Your team focuses on strategic compliance initiatives, instead of spending all their time chasing evidence.

3️⃣ Price

With traditional audit firms, the grunt work and manual processes tend to get passed to you.

Our approach? Tech + expertise.
-Higher-quality audits
-Fewer surprises
-Lower total cost

With Thoropass, you get an audit partner who owns the busywork and burden, not one who hands it back to you.

Risk reduction driven by accuracy, context, and expertise — not procedural box-ticking.

At the end of the day, audits are about human judgment.

Technology partnered with the right audit expertise can accelerate that judgment. 

That’s the audit partner your business actually needs.

915195233.body:
Ignore the EU AI Act at Your Own Risk. 

The European Commission has made it clear: enforcement of the AI Act is moving forward with no delays. For U.S. companies serving European customers, this isn’t a distant policy issue—it’s a business reality.

To support compliance, the Commission recently introduced three tools for providers of general-purpose AI (GPAI) models:

Guidelines – clarify who must comply
Code of Practice – practical guidance on transparency, copyright, and safety
Template – summarize the data used to train AI models

Together, these tools reduce administrative burden, foster innovation, and build public trust.

For U.S. companies, the implications are clear:

Prove compliance with international standards
Restrict or adjust AI features in certain regions
Document and disclose training data in line with EU expectations

The takeaway: U.S. regulation won’t be the only factor shaping your AI strategy. European policy will impact you directly.

👉 The best way to prepare is by understanding and documenting your AI controls today—so you’re ready when enforcement arrives.

Learn more about how Thoropass delivers enterprise-grade audits at AI-native speed here: https://www.thoropass.com/

915136283.body:
In today’s environment, CISOs are being asked to do more—with less.

915096473.body:
Ignore the EU AI Act at Your Own Risk. 

The European Commission has made it clear: enforcement of the AI Act is moving forward with no delays. For U.S. companies serving European customers, this isn’t a distant policy issue—it’s a business reality.

To support compliance, the Commission recently introduced three tools for providers of general-purpose AI (GPAI) models:

Guidelines – clarify who must comply
Code of Practice – practical guidance on transparency, copyright, and safety
Template – summarize the data used to train AI models

Together, these tools reduce administrative burden, foster innovation, and build public trust.

For U.S. companies, the implications are clear:

Prove compliance with international standards
Restrict or adjust AI features in certain regions
Document and disclose training data in line with EU expectations

The takeaway: U.S. regulation won’t be the only factor shaping your AI strategy. European policy will impact you directly.

👉 The best way to prepare is by understanding and documenting your AI controls today—so you’re ready when enforcement arrives.

1195038753.body:
Compliance has been broken for a long time - not because of the tools, but because of the model.

Every wave of GRC improved how we track trust work: spreadsheets → software → automation → dashboards. But underneath, the system still relies on people to notice changes, interpret them, chase them down, and close the loop.

We normalized that. Built teams around it. Called it process.

But it doesn’t really hold up anymore.

The surface area keeps expanding. Frameworks multiply. Vendors move fast. AI shows up before it’s assessed. And inside most companies, someone is still rebuilding evidence, chasing approvals, answering the same questions… over and over.

Trust doesn’t usually fail in one big moment. It drifts. Quietly. Until someone asks for proof; and it’s not there.

There’s a different way this can work.

Systems that understand change as it happens.
Systems that do the work instead of just reporting on it.
Humans focused on decisions, not coordination.

Where staying trustworthy is the default, not something you scramble for.

Automation helped reduce clicks. It didn’t reduce the underlying burden.
Autonomy is a different shift - it changes what the system can actually do.

We’ve been spending time building toward that idea.

We’re calling it an Autonomous Trust Platform - but more importantly, it’s a rethink of how trust operations should work going forward.

This is the shift we've been building towards. And we're just getting started.
→ sprinto.com

1404490216.body:
Enterprise risk is never static. So why are we using static GRC tools?

In the traditional GRC setup, you have all your risks listed with ratings inside the platform. Your risk analyst logs in to go through them and figure out what needs a deeper dig.

The analyst needs situational awareness to tie all the pieces together and give you the right insight.

In the legacy platforms, your team only gets a static snapshot of your environment, which means you only see what was caught at that moment.

Here's what's different when you have an AI agent at your fingertips:

✨ Continuous monitoring means you know what's happening in engineering right now and where controls are falling short
💡  Deep data processing means you can compare policies against what is actually being followed
🌎 Context across data sources means you can surface risks you haven’t even thought to document in your registry yet

AI is not smarter than your human analyst. It just doesn't have to choose what to pay attention to, the processing power is the real unlock, the agent is watching everything, all the time.

That’s the direction we're heading at Vanta.

The AI agent that looks at all the data, gives you visibility into a constantly changing environment and tells you where something's missing, helping you along the way to prioritize the right risks and make the right decisions. 

As we figure out how to extend trust wisely to AI and allow it to power the security team the same way it's powering the rest of the organization.....we enable a new approach to enterprise risk management. 

Check out more of what we are up to here: https://lnkd.in/e2b8SBuM

1400156356.body:
I am super pumped to announce that AARM (Autonomous Action Runtime Management) has been donated to the Cloud Security Alliance.

This is a life-defining moment for me.

What started as a technical product research effort at Vanta turned into something much bigger (this is also thanks to Boris Logvinsky and Jeremy Epling for setting me up for success). I uncovered a gap in how we secure AI agent actions at runtime, defined the problem, and published a specification that created an entirely new system category.

Less than 80 days from launch:

→ 42 companies are building around the AARM specification
→ 3 companies have achieved AARM conformance
→ A Technical Working Group with dozens of contributors is actively shaping the standard
→ An Agent running conformance reviews without human intervention

And then I met Jim Reavis and Daniele Catteddu at RSAC and they offered to bring AARM into their newly launched CSAI Foundation Agentic Security initiative, not as observers, but as research contributors and working group co-chairs.

I didn't set out to build a standard. I set out to solve a problem. But when a category emerges from real need, and an ecosystem forms around it this quickly, you have a responsibility to give it the right home.

CSA is that home. They bring the governance, the global reach, and the standards infrastructure that AARM needs to scale beyond what any individual could sustain.

To the 42 companies in our ecosystem, the TWG members who challenged and improved the spec, and everyone who took a chance on an arXiv paper from an unknown PM: thank you. This is your milestone as much as mine.

The specification is open. The mission hasn't changed. We're just getting started.

p.s. I am recruiting the Co-chair of the AARM working group at CSA, if you are interested, hit me up with a DM.

Thanks to all the key people that have really made the difference, I will tag more in the comments, as LI does not like too many mentions: Phil Venables, Gal Moyal, Tal Peretz, Mokhtar Bacha, Chase Lee, Jiquan Ngiam, Oleksandr Yaremchuk, Niv Braun, Dor Sarig, Daniel Morris, Chris Sestito Ankush Jain David Girvin Sharath Rajasekar Vrajesh B. Elad Schulman Mathijs van der Maas Matthew Rosenquist Camille Stewart Gloster Ken Huang Anthony Scarfe Hema Kak Kalsi Alex Foley Kavya Pearlman Krti T. 1396908356.body:
40 security companies reached the same conclusion: agents need stronger runtime security around agentic actions.

Here's why this matters: agents don't just generate text anymore, they take actions, call tools, and make decisions at machine speed. Traditional security tools weren't built for this because they can’t understand agent intent or context.

That means we need a new type of system that intercepts agent actions, evaluates them with full session context, and approves them before execution, all while maintaining strong observability and auditing.

This gap requires a new system category that we named: AARM (Autonomous Action Runtime Management).

I coined AARM through product research at Vanta, and we're releasing the specification to the community openly because categories aren't defined by one vendor, they're defined by the people building.

On April 30, I'm taking the stage at the Cloud Security Alliance Agentic AI Security Summit to introduce AARM as a new system category, alongside the 39 companies independently building in this space who are now coming together as part of the AARM community.

I'll also share a major announcement that day.

If you're building, buying, testing, or breaking agents, this is the room you want to be in.

Register: https://lnkd.in/gBNnc6Up

770239713.body:
When I led compliance at Citi, I always had access to deep compliance and IT risk expertise. 

Local and global teams worked together seamlessly. If a new regulatory requirement surfaced in Singapore, we had the talent to assess it, develop a plan, and align stakeholders.

That level of embedded expertise is rare. Most businesses don’t have the luxury of standing compliance teams across regions, or the resources to support full-time specialists in every framework or geography.

That’s why the human expertise behind Thoropass is so important.

We don’t just automate compliance workflows. We embed expert guidance into the process, so our customers have access to the insight they need—when they need it.

Every company is different: tech stacks, data flows, customer types, and risk profiles vary widely. But access to trusted compliance expertise shouldn’t be a barrier to growth.

With Thoropass it’s not.

We deliver a powerful combination: modern compliance automation and on-demand human expertise. It’s how we help businesses stay compliant, stay protected, and move forward with confidence.

If you're interested in learning more, you can book some time with one of our experts here: https://lnkd.in/evX8_hPU
